title: Receive offers - Verify offers | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Apply the phased offer compliance process
Determine if a peer review is required

structure_blocks.heading: Verify offers
structure_blocks.text: Verify offers

structure_blocks.heading: Verify offers
structure_blocks.text: Verifying offers ensures that all required documents and certifications are accurate and complete, maintaining the integrity of the procurement process.

structure_blocks.heading: Certifications, declarations, and proofs
structure_blocks.text: Certifications, declarations, and proofs

structure_blocks.heading: Certifications, declarations, and proofs
structure_blocks.text: Certifications, declarations, and proofs are standalone documents that substantiate information submitted with an offer. Standalone documents are mandatory to finalize the evaluation process. Personal references may be included provided they do not alter the offer's substance or introduce information that could improve a score or ranking.

structure_blocks.heading: Certifications, declarations, and proofs
structure_blocks.text: You may request mandatory certifications, declarations, or proof documents at offer closing. However, you must not require offerors to provide these documents at offer closing unless the documentation being requested is included in the Exceptions list . You must ensure that all solicitation documents identify which certifications, declarations and proofs are required.

structure_blocks.heading: Certifications, declarations, and proofs
structure_blocks.text: Should these documents be absent at the solicitation of offers’ closing, you will notify the offeror who then must submit the missing documents within two business days following this notification.

structure_blocks.heading: Certifications, declarations, and proofs
structure_blocks.text: You will inform offerors who fail to provide the required documents within the two-business-day timeframe that their offer is non-responsive and that it will be given no further consideration.

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: These exceptions outline specific cases where additional certifications, declarations, or proof documents are required at the time of offer submission.

structure_blocks.heading: Integrity Provisions
structure_blocks.text: Integrity Provisions

structure_blocks.heading: Integrity Provisions
structure_blocks.text: The offeror must provide a completed Declaration Form or a list of directors or owners in accordance with conditions defined in Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Businesses (PSIB)
structure_blocks.text: Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Businesses (PSIB)

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Businesses (PSIB)
structure_blocks.text: Offerors must include with their offer certification that they meet the PSIB definition of an Indigenous Business.

structure_blocks.heading: Canadian Content Policy
structure_blocks.text: Canadian Content Policy

structure_blocks.heading: Canadian Content Policy
structure_blocks.text: The offeror must provide certification in accordance with conditions defined in the Canadian Content Policy.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial capability documentation
structure_blocks.text: Buyers, in consultation with the Cost and Price Analysis Group, determine what financial information will be required from a subset of offerors during the offer evaluation phase and the time frame for providing the information.

structure_blocks.heading: Solicitations using Public Openings
structure_blocks.text: The offeror must provide certifications, declarations, and proofs with the offer as specified in the solicitation.

structure_blocks.text: Verification of offeror’s certification

structure_blocks.heading: Verification of offeror’s certification
structure_blocks.text: As the buyer, it is not your responsibility to verify the validity of the offeror's certifications for procurements set-aside under the PSIB . If you question the validity of a certification, the particulars must be referred to the Business Development Directorate, Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) for audit by third party auditors.

structure_blocks.text: The authority to reject an offer rests with you except in the case of offers being considered for rejection in the following circumstances where Director General approval is required:

structure_blocks.heading: Authority to reject offers
structure_blocks.text: When you intend to reject an offer, you must inform the offeror verbally by telephone call. The telephone call serves as the official notification. You should record the date, time of the call, and the person spoken to while also stating the reasons for rejection.

structure_blocks.heading: Authority to reject offers
structure_blocks.text: You must also provide written confirmation of the rejection setting out the facts and the reasons for the decision to reject the offer.

structure_blocks.heading: Authority to reject offers
structure_blocks.text: Review and challenge process: Offerors, excluded as per the instructions in the procurement guidelines, have the right to request a review by the Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) responsible for the procurement if their offer is rejected. The decision to pause the evaluation and contract award process to allow additional time for this review lies solely with the ADM .

structure_blocks.heading: Authority to reject offers
structure_blocks.text: A review by the ADM will result in an investigation and a decision will be made. This decision could impact not just the specific procurement in question but potentially broader procurement practices. Offerors will be notified in writing of the review outcome.

structure_blocks.text: You must immediately alert Legal Services and their immediate director whenever there is an indication of possible bid-rigging activities, collusion, or fraud. When necessary, Legal Services will facilitate discussions with Competition Bureau Canada, a federal independent law enforcement agency responsible for the Competition Act. Bid rigging is specifically addressed under section 47 of the Competition Act .

structure_blocks.text: The following are examples of possible bid-rigging activities:

structure_blocks.heading: Bid rigging, collusion, fraud
structure_blocks.text: offer rates/prices are much higher than published price lists, engineering cost estimates, or previous offer rates/prices by the same suppliers for no apparent reason

raw_text:
Verify offers

Verifying offers ensures that all required documents and certifications are accurate and complete, maintaining the integrity of the procurement process.

Certifications, declarations, and proofs

Certifications, declarations, and proofs are standalone documents that substantiate information submitted with an offer. Standalone documents are mandatory to finalize the evaluation process. Personal references may be included provided they do not alter the offer's substance or introduce information that could improve a score or ranking.

You may request mandatory certifications, declarations, or proof documents at offer closing. However, you must not require offerors to provide these documents at offer closing unless the documentation being requested is included in the Exceptions list . You must ensure that all solicitation documents identify which certifications, declarations and proofs are required.

Should these documents be absent at the solicitation of offers’ closing, you will notify the offeror who then must submit the missing documents within two business days following this notification.

You will inform offerors who fail to provide the required documents within the two-business-day timeframe that their offer is non-responsive and that it will be given no further consideration.

Exceptions

These exceptions outline specific cases where additional certifications, declarations, or proof documents are required at the time of offer submission.

Integrity Provisions

The offeror must provide a completed Declaration Form or a list of directors or owners in accordance with conditions defined in Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Businesses (PSIB)

Offerors must include with their offer certification that they meet the PSIB definition of an Indigenous Business.

Canadian Content Policy

The offeror must provide certification in accordance with conditions defined in the Canadian Content Policy.

Registry and Pre-qualification of Floating Plant

The offeror must provide certification with the offer.

Financial capability documentation

Buyers, in consultation with the Cost and Price Analysis Group, determine what financial information will be required from a subset of offerors during the offer evaluation phase and the time frame for providing the information.

Solicitations using Public Openings

The offeror must provide certifications, declarations, and proofs with the offer as specified in the solicitation.

Verification of offeror’s certification

As the buyer, it is not your responsibility to verify the validity of the offeror's certifications for procurements set-aside under the PSIB . If you question the validity of a certification, the particulars must be referred to the Business Development Directorate, Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) for audit by third party auditors.

Managing offers under vendor performance and corrective measures

When managing offers under vendor performance and corrective measures (VPCM), consider the following:

you must not evaluate offers received from an offeror if the offeror is subject to a vendor performance and corrective measures (VPCM) relevant to the procurement (offers include offers for contracts under supply arrangements)

you must set aside any standing offer or supply arrangement for vendors under a relevant VPCM

you may reject offers due to a VPCM at any point up to the contract award or issuance of a standing offer or supply arrangement

check the Vendor Information Management (VIM) system for a VPCM at closing for competitive solicitations and before contact for sole sourcing. Recheck VIM before awarding a contract or issuing a standing offer or supply arrangement

when accessing the VIM file on an offeror, you will have a clear notice of any VPCM . It is up to you to verify whether the vendor is subject to a VPCM

Authority to reject offers

The authority to reject an offer rests with you except in the case of offers being considered for rejection in the following circumstances where Director General approval is required:

bankruptcy or inoperability of the offeror

evidence of fraud, bribery, misrepresentation, or failure to comply with anti-discrimination laws

prior contractual remedies exercised by Canada, such as suspension or termination for default

poor performance on previous contracts jeopardizing successful completion of the current offer

When you intend to reject an offer, you must inform the offeror verbally by telephone call. The telephone call serves as the official notification. You should record the date, time of the call, and the person spoken to while also stating the reasons for rejection.

You must also provide written confirmation of the rejection setting out the facts and the reasons for the decision to reject the offer.

Review and challenge process: Offerors, excluded as per the instructions in the procurement guidelines, have the right to request a review by the Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) responsible for the procurement if their offer is rejected. The decision to pause the evaluation and contract award process to allow additional time for this review lies solely with the ADM .

A review by the ADM will result in an investigation and a decision will be made. This decision could impact not just the specific procurement in question but potentially broader procurement practices. Offerors will be notified in writing of the review outcome.

Bid rigging, collusion, fraud

You must immediately alert Legal Services and their immediate director whenever there is an indication of possible bid-rigging activities, collusion, or fraud. When necessary, Legal Services will facilitate discussions with Competition Bureau Canada, a federal independent law enforcement agency responsible for the Competition Act. Bid rigging is specifically addressed under section 47 of the Competition Act .

The following are examples of possible bid-rigging activities:

offer rates/prices are much higher than published price lists, engineering cost estimates, or previous offer rates/prices by the same suppliers for no apparent reason

winning offerors subcontracting to those with higher offers on the same project

identical descriptions for non-standard items by different offerors

indications of unusual pre-offer communications among competitors about pricing or market division before offer submission

a single offeror's consistent success under specific conditions

a pattern of offer rotation among a group of offerors
